preFix.title: Repairing a rotted door bottom rail
preFix.subtitle: Cut out soft timber from the bottom rail of a wooden door and repair with a two-part epoxy filler
preFix.excerpt: How to repair localised rot in the bottom rail of a wooden external door without replacing the door. Covers probing the rot extent, cutting back to sound timber, applying a wood hardener, and building back up with two-part epoxy filler.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Traditional UK joinery repair practice.

content.text: The bottom rail of an external door takes more moisture exposure than any other part of the frame, water pools on the threshold and wicks up the end grain. Localised rot caught early can be repaired with
epoxy filler

for a fraction of the cost of a new door. Rot that has spread beyond the rail into the stiles or has caused structural splitting across the joint needs a joiner to assess whether replacement is the better repair.
Step 1, Assess the rot extent

Probe the soft area with a penknife blade or the tip of the utility knife. Soft, fibrous timber that the blade enters with no resistance is active or recently dried-out rot. Mark the boundary of the affected area. Probe beyond the visible damage, rot often travels further through the end grain than the surface suggests.
Step 2, Remove all soft timber

Using the chisel and mallet, cut away all soft and discoloured timber, working back to sound wood. The repair only holds if it is bonded to sound timber throughout. Do not try to be conservative with the removal, sound timber is dark, uniform, and resists the chisel. Soft timber is pale, fibrous, and crumbles. Brush out all loose material.
Step 3, Dry out the timber

If the timber is damp, leave the repair area open to dry in a warm room or in still air for at least 3 days before applying the hardener. Applying wood hardener to wet timber traps moisture under the repair. Point a small fan heater at the area if the weather is damp.
Step 4, Apply wood hardener
Apply two coats of
wood hardener

to the remaining porous timber using the paint brush. Allow each coat to soak in fully and become dry and firm before mixing the filler. Ronseal, Selleys, and Everbuild all produce compatible hardener-and-filler systems, use hardener and filler from the same manufacturer.
Step 5, Mix and apply the filler

Mix the two-part epoxy filler according to the instructions, typically equal volumes of resin and hardener, kneaded or stirred together until the colour is uniform. Apply promptly: most two-part fillers have a working time of 10 to 15 minutes before they begin to stiffen. Press the filler firmly into the repair, slightly overfilling the void. Shape roughly with the scraper. Allow to cure fully, typically 1 to 2 hours at room temperature; overnight in cold weather.
Step 6, Shape and prime

Once fully cured, epoxy filler sets harder than most softwoods. Sand or file to shape with 80-grit paper on the sanding block, working to match the door profile. Prime the repair immediately with a solvent-based exterior wood primer, epoxy filler is not fully weatherproof without a primer and paint coat over it. Paint to match.
Variations
Deep void spanning more than 30 mm of the rail thickness: fill in two layers, allowing the first to cure before applying the second. Single deep fills can be difficult to work to shape in time.

Preventing recurrence: seal the repaired bottom rail edge with two coats of exterior primer and two coats of gloss on every accessible surface, including the bottom edge. Fit a rain deflector strip on the outer sill if there isn't one already.
Troubleshooter

items.fix: Remove the filler, reapply in two layers.
items.cause: Filler components not mixed in the correct ratio, or applied too thick in one pass
items.symptom: Filler cracks or shrinks after setting

items.fix: Remove, dry thoroughly, apply wood hardener, and re-fill.
items.cause: Wood hardener not applied, or timber still wet when the filler went on
items.symptom: Filler lifts away from the timber

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
paragraph reads at grade 11.0 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
